HomeGlobal ShocksSingaporeRegional

Regional

Asian economies exposed to Middle East stress: financial markets and country-level monitoring

Energy supply

Overview

Price pass-through is sharply bifurcated: the Philippines (+5.6 pp headline vs pre-war levels) and Thailand (+3.3 pp) are absorbing energy costs into consumer prices at a pace well above the rest of the region, while China, Indonesia, and Japan show little or no acceleration. Shipping tells a similarly split story — South Korea's tanker import tonnage is running 36% below pre-conflict norms and total port calls 22% short, while Thailand's tanker arrivals are running 101% above pre-conflict norms, suggesting active rerouting and stockpiling rather than uniform disruption. Industrial metals (copper +27%, aluminium +29%, nickel +26% vs pre-war levels) and JKM LNG (+59%) confirm that upstream cost pressure is broad-based and sustained, even as coal and palm oil sit well off their conflict-period highs.

Key Drivers (5)

  • Philippines headline CPI has surged to 7.2% YoY in April 2026, +5.6 pp above the November–December 2025 average, with core CPI also up +1.5 pp to 3.9% — the most acute price pass-through in the region. Thailand headline CPI has swung from -0.4% to +2.9% (+3.3 pp), though core remains contained at 0.8%, suggesting the move is predominantly energy-driven.

    ⌗ rg.prices.philippines⌗ rg.prices.thailand

  • Vietnam headline CPI is at 5.5% YoY (+1.9 pp vs pre-war levels) with core at 4.7% (+1.4 pp), indicating that energy cost pressure is feeding through into underlying prices — the broadest core acceleration in the region. India headline CPI is at 3.5% (+2.7 pp vs pre-war levels) but core has actually fallen 1.1 pp to 3.4%, suggesting the headline move reflects food/energy rather than generalised inflation.

    ⌗ rg.prices.vietnam⌗ rg.prices.india

  • South Korea's tanker port calls are 35% below pre-conflict norms (4-week average gap -22%), with tanker import tonnage 36% short and export tonnage 44% short — the most severe energy-shipping shortfall in the region and still deteriorating. Japan's tanker import tonnage is 14% below pre-conflict norms on a 4-week average basis, and tanker export tonnage is 35% short on the latest week, consistent with refinery throughput constraints.

    ⌗ rg.shipping.kr.tanker⌗ rg.shipping.jp.tanker

  • Thailand's tanker port calls are running 34% above pre-conflict norms (4-week average +23%) and tanker import tonnage is 101% above — far exceeding normal seasonal levels — suggesting aggressive front-loading of energy imports. Indonesia's tanker calls are 7% below pre-conflict norms on a 4-week average, consistent with modest but sustained supply tightness given Indonesia sources 53% of its refined petroleum from Singapore.

    ⌗ rg.shipping.th.tanker⌗ rg.shipping.id.tanker⌗ rg.trade.fuel.regional_fuel_share_comparison

  • JKM LNG is at $17.0/MMBtu, up 59% from the November–December 2025 average and still well above pre-conflict levels despite pulling back from the conflict-period high of $22.4. Copper (+27%), aluminium (+29%), and nickel (+26%) are all at or near their conflict-period highs, signalling broad-based upstream cost pressure on industrial and energy inputs across the region.

    ⌗ rg.financial_markets.jkm_lng⌗ rg.financial_markets.copper⌗ rg.financial_markets.aluminum⌗ rg.financial_markets.nickel
Data Gaps (5)
  • All IPI series except Vietnam are stale (data age 73 days), limiting visibility into whether industrial output is responding to energy cost pressure in the most recent weeks.
  • Indonesia IPI last observation is December 2025 (163 days old), predating the conflict entirely — no war-period industrial activity signal available for Indonesia.
  • All Comtrade refined-petroleum and industrial-chemical share data are annual 2024 figures (498 days old); Vietnam Comtrade data is 2023 vintage (864 days old), materially limiting assessment of current Singapore-dependence exposure for Vietnam.
  • All SingStats monthly trade flow series (fuel and chemical imports from Singapore) are stale at 73 days, covering only through March 2026 — no April data available to assess post-escalation trade flows.
  • Newcastle coal and crude palm oil series have no pre-war baseline computed (baseline window returns null), preventing delta-vs-baseline quantification for those commodities.

Financial markets

Overview

Regional sovereign bond yields are under moderate upward pressure, led by the Philippines (+102 bp vs pre-war levels) and Indonesia (+58 bp), tracking a US 10-year yield that has risen 35 bp to a conflict-period high of 4.46%. FX moves remain relatively contained — most currencies are within ±5% of pre-war levels — but the CNY is at its weakest point since the conflict began (-4.1% vs pre-war levels) and the IDR and PHP have depreciated meaningfully. Gold at $4,699/oz is 12% above pre-war levels but has retreated 11% from its conflict-period peak of $5,294, suggesting safe-haven demand has moderated from its March extremes.

Key Drivers (3)

  • The Philippines 10-year yield has risen to 6.86%, up +102 bp from the pre-war average of 5.84% — the largest sovereign yield move in the region — consistent with the country's acute inflation shock. Indonesia's 10-year yield is at 6.78%, up +58 bp, and Vietnam's is up +52 bp to 4.21% near its conflict-period high. Thailand is up +44 bp to 2.16%. Malaysia (+9 bp) is the notable outlier, with yields barely moved.

    ⌗ rg.financial_markets.sovereign_10_year_bond_yields_per_annum

  • The CNY has depreciated to its weakest level since the conflict began, now 4.1% below the November–December 2025 average and at a conflict-period low. The IDR has weakened 4.7% and the PHP 4.4% vs pre-war levels. The MYR is down 4.7%. By contrast, the VND is essentially flat (-0.06%) and the JPY has moved only +1.5%, reflecting managed or anchored exchange rate regimes.

    ⌗ rg.financial_markets.currency_strength_vs_usd_indexed_2026_01_01_100

  • Gold at $4,699/oz is 12% above the pre-war average, confirming sustained safe-haven demand, but has pulled back 11% from the conflict-period peak of $5,294 reached on 2 March 2026. The 4-week trend is -2.1%, suggesting the acute safe-haven spike has partially unwound even as underlying geopolitical risk persists.

    ⌗ rg.financial_markets.gold
Data Gaps (3)
  • Philippines 10-year bond yield data is stale at 15 days (last observation 28 April 2026) and has only 4 war-period data points, limiting confidence in the yield-level reading.
  • No sovereign yield data available for China, Japan, South Korea, or Taiwan in this dashboard snapshot, leaving the largest regional bond markets unobserved.
  • No SGD, KRW, or TWD FX series are present in the currency index chart, limiting coverage of key regional currencies.
Zoom

Inflation

Year-on-year inflation across 10 Asian economies (China, India, Indonesia, Japan, Malaysia, Philippines, South Korea, Taiwan, Thailand, Vietnam). Each chart shows headline CPI and core CPI side-by-side: headline captures the broadest pass-through of the Iran/Hormuz energy shock; core strips out food and energy to expose second-round effects.

Industrial Production

Industrial / manufacturing production across 10 Asian economies. Aligns with the Singapore Sectoral IPI format so cross-country trends can be read on a single comparable scale.

⌗ rg.sectoral_activity.china

India

Regional dependence on Singapore — by product

Use the dropdown to switch between two product views. Industrial chemicals capture Singapore's value-add chemical exports; refined petroleum captures Singapore's role as the regional refining hub for Middle East crude. Indonesia, Malaysia, and Thailand are most exposed in both, but the magnitude differs sharply — single-digit shares for chemicals versus 30–50% for refined petroleum.

Refined petroleum imports from Singapore — annual SG shares by regional country

How dependent each regional economy is on Singapore for refined-petroleum imports — SITC 334 (gasoline, diesel, jet fuel, naphtha, fuel oil). Indirect channel through which Middle East crude disruption reaches the region via Singapore's refining hub. Ordered by 2024 share.

⌗ rg.trade.fuel.regional_fuel_share_comparison

Refined petroleum imports from Singapore — monthly levels by regional country

Monthly absolute imports of refined petroleum (SITC 334) from Singapore in SGD thousands, with the 2023–24 monthly average as a dashed reference line. Cards ordered by 2024 Singapore share, descending.

Industrial chemical imports from Singapore — annual SG shares by regional country

How dependent each regional economy is on Singapore for industrial-chemical imports — SITC 5 less SITC 51 (organics) less SITC 54 (pharmaceuticals), the chemicals subset most exposed to Middle East upstream cost pressure. Ordered by 2024 share.

⌗ rg.trade.chem.regional_chem_share_comparison

Monthly absolute imports of industrial chemicals (SITC 5 less 51 less 54) from Singapore in SGD thousands, with the 2023–24 monthly average as a dashed reference line. Cards ordered by 2024 Singapore share, descending.

Regional shipping nowcast

Same shipping nowcast cards as the Singapore tab, shown one regional country at a time. Pick a country from the selector to switch the view. Source: IMF PortWatch satellite data, processed by the MAS shipping nowcast pipeline.

China port calls — overview

China's total weekly port calls — actual vs the counterfactual estimate of what calls would have been absent the war.

Total port calls (all vessel types, imports + exports)

Weekly aggregate of all vessel types arriving at this country's ports. Solid blue is actual; dashed amber is the counterfactual primary estimate.

⌗ rg.shipping.cn.total_port_calls_all_vessel_types_imports_exports

China shipping activity — by vessel type

Vessel-type drill-down for the two categories most directly exposed to Iran-related disruption: tankers (energy trade) and containers (general goods). Each card shows three weekly metrics — port calls (vessel count), import tonnage (cargo unloaded), and export tonnage (cargo loaded).

Exchange rates

Daily Asian currency rates against USD, rebased to 100 at 2026-01-01 so currencies with very different magnitudes can share an axis. Higher = local currency weaker vs USD. Source: yfinance.

Currency strength vs USD (indexed, 2026-01-01 = 100)

⌗ rg.financial_markets.currency_strength_vs_usd_indexed_2026_01_01_100

Bond yields

Daily 10-year government bond yields, % per annum. US from yfinance (^TNX); ASEAN-4 + Vietnam from CEIC.

Sovereign 10-year bond yields (% per annum)

⌗ rg.financial_markets.sovereign_10_year_bond_yields_per_annum

Commodity prices

Key commodity benchmarks relevant to Asian trade and energy exposure. yfinance for COMEX/ICE-listed contracts (Gold, Copper, LME Aluminum); investing.com for niche regional benchmarks (LME Nickel, FCPO MYR, JKM LNG, Newcastle coal, rubber TSR20).

Gold

COMEX gold futures front-month (USD/oz).

⌗ rg.financial_markets.gold

Copper

COMEX copper futures (USD/lb).

⌗ rg.financial_markets.copper

Aluminum

LME aluminum 3-month forward (USD/tonne).

⌗ rg.financial_markets.aluminum

Nickel

LME nickel (USD/tonne). Used in stainless steel and EV batteries; Indonesia is the largest producer and SE Asia a major refiner.

⌗ rg.financial_markets.nickel

JKM LNG

Japan/Korea Marker (USD/MMBtu). The Asian spot LNG benchmark assessed daily by Platts. Qatari LNG accounts for ~20% of global supply.

⌗ rg.financial_markets.jkm_lng

Rubber TSR20

Bangkok STR 20 (≡ TSR 20), 2nd-month FOB price, converted to USc/kg via daily FX. Asian natural rubber benchmark — Thailand leads regional production, with Indonesia and Vietnam close behind.

⌗ rg.financial_markets.rubber_tsr20

Newcastle coal

Newcastle FOB Australia thermal coal (USD/tonne). The seaborne Asian thermal coal benchmark.

⌗ rg.financial_markets.newcastle_coal

Crude palm oil

FCPO front-month on Bursa Malaysia (MYR/tonne). The global palm oil benchmark; Indonesia and Malaysia are the top exporters.

⌗ rg.financial_markets.crude_palm_oil

MAS EPG reports

Middle East Faultline Watch

ME Faultline Watch (“The Watch”) is a joint initiative by IED and FMS to identify countries most exposed to energy and/or financial stress arising from the Middle East conflict. The Watch focuses on economies with the weakest links—those facing heightened external spillovers amid limited energy and financial buffers—where shocks from higher energy prices, tighter financial conditions, or disrupted flows are most likely to translate into macro-financial vulnerabilities.

ME Faultline Watch — Philippines
